posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: My Brother's American Adventure (and How I Survived Without Him)
posts.excerpt: While my brother was busy living the American dream—from soccer in Arkansas to Spring Break in Mexico—I was back home in Poland navigating the bittersweet reality of 8th-grade stress, tricky time zones for gaming, and the glorious (yet lonely) luxury of having two bedrooms all to myself.
posts.body: My older brother went on a high school exchange to the USA when he was 17. He lived in Arkansas, attended an American high school, and even played for the school soccer team. During his exchange, he traveled a lot – he visited Los Angeles, Florida, Las Vegas, and went on a cruise to Mexico for Spring Break! To be honest, when he left, I didn’t care that much. I didn’t even say a proper goodbye. Just as I expected, the time flew by really fast, but there were moments when it slowed down and I really missed him. I am used to being alone, but this time I felt truly lonely. Knowing he was just a phone call away wasn't the same. I missed his support the most when I was in the 8th grade. I was extremely stressed about my final exams. My parents were there for me, but my brother had already passed these exams and could have given me the best advice. Somehow, I managed to do it all without him and I survived! We sometimes called each other to play video games together. However, it wasn't easy because of the 7-hour time difference. When it was 7:00 PM here in Poland, it was only noon in Arkansas. Since he had his own school schedule, we could only play on weekends or holidays. But there was one huge advantage to his absence! I had both of our rooms entirely to myself. I used one room as my gaming and studying area, and the other one just for sleeping and keeping my things. It honestly made my life so much easier during that time! Here is a picture of my brother with his friends from his favorite AP History class. You can see his school building in the background. This is the amazing view from his Spring Break cruise. Just look at this beautiful beach in Mexico! And this is him in Las Vegas, standing in front of the famous Sphere with his host Mom. The best part is that she is coming to visit us in Warsaw this summer!

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: A Bittersweet Birthday at the Championships
posts.excerpt: On June 28th, which happened to be my birthday, I took part in one of the most important competitions of my life. It was the Polish Championships in climbing, held in Bytom.
posts.body: On June 28th, which happened to be my birthday, I took part in one of the most important competitions of my life. It was the Polish Championships in climbing, held in Bytom. I traveled there the day before with my dad, my brother, and Ziemek, another climber competing in my junior category. Ziemek and I were lucky to get free accommodation in a hotel thanks to funding for athletes. On Friday evening, my brother, Ziemek, and I went for a walk to meet other competitors and my coach, who were staying in a different hotel. The path led through a forest, and the late June weather was perfect for an evening walk. A beautiful sunset view during our walk to see the coach. When we met the coach, we talked about the competition. He advised me not to worry too much and not to stress because stress would only make things worse. Despite his advice, I was very stressed the next day. I tried my best during the bouldering competition, but unfortunately, it seemed I wasn't prepared enough yet. Out of 5 boulders, I managed to complete only 3 and reach one "zone". I ended up in 18th place. To get to the finals, you need to be in the top 8, so I didn't qualify this time. This is me during the competition, trying my hardest on the wall. The next day, I had another competition, this time in lead climbing. Things didn't go according to plan either. Halfway up the first route, my foot slipped, even though I felt I could have climbed it to the very top. On the second route, I couldn't focus properly because I was still thinking about my mistake on the first one. It ruined my chances of getting into the finals again. Sad after the start. I knew I could have done much better. It was a tough birthday weekend, but it was also a valuable experience for future competitions.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: Epic Summer Road Trip: Rocks, Glaciers, and a Close Call!
posts.excerpt: Early in July 2025, my dad, my brother, and I went on an amazing summer road trip. Our first stop was Zillertal in Austria. We camped there for two days because it was very close to the climbing area.
posts.body: Early in July 2025, my dad, my brother, and I went on an amazing summer road trip. Our first stop was Zillertal in Austria. We camped there for two days because it was very close to the climbing area. I wanted to try a boulder called "King of Queens" (grade 7b+). The rocks were right next to a river, and getting to them was a real challenge! The river was high and freezing cold. We had to figure out how to carry our backpacks and crash pads across the water without getting them wet. My dad and brother didn't feel like climbing that day, so I was trying the boulder alone. After about ten attempts, I finally did it! It is still my best outdoor bouldering send ever, and I am really proud of it. Me taking a well-deserved rest on the grass in Zillertal after sending the boulder. Next, we drove to Switzerland to a famous climbing area called Magic Wood. I spent two days there trying a boulder called "Intermezzo". On the first day, I felt great. The conditions were good, and I figured out all the moves, but I just couldn't link them together to finish the whole route. I came back the next day full of hope, but the conditions were terrible. Because it's such a popular place, the holds were super slippery. I kept falling off, and sadly, I had to give up because we had to travel to our next destination. A cool hotel building we saw while sightseeing in St. Moritz. After a relaxing day in St. Moritz, we went for a huge hike up the Diavolezza mountain (2978 meters above sea level). The hike was long and tiring, but the views were totally worth it. Here is a picture from our hike up the rocky ridge of Diavolezza. When we were close to the top, something really scary happened. A thunderstorm was approaching. My hair under my helmet started to stand up from static electricity, I suddenly got a headache, and the rocks around us began making a weird buzzing sound. At first, I didn't know what it meant, but my dad realized we were in huge danger. We quickly rushed to the summit and then started running down. We descended safely by walking across a glacier back to the trail. Descending the mountain via the glacier. It was an intense experience! The next day, we crossed the border into Italy to see the beautiful Lake Como. A beautiful view of Lake Como with a classic Italian boat. We also walked through these charming, empty Italian streets at night. In the evening, we went to Italian thermal baths. After all that bouldering and running away from a storm, relaxing in the hot water was exactly what we needed. The day after that, we started our journey back home to Poland. My brother and I chilling in the glowing blue water of the thermal baths.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: A European Road Trip: City Sights and Bouldering Goals
posts.excerpt: In the middle of August, my mom, my brother, my sister, and I went on a road trip across a few European countries.
posts.body: In the middle of August, my mom, my brother, my sister, and I went on a road trip across a few European countries. Our first stop was Amsterdam in the Netherlands. To be honest, I didn't expect the city to smell so... strange! However, I really liked the city itself. Its concept built on water canals reminded me of Venice. Another crazy thing was the number of cyclists – there were bikes absolutely everywhere! Just look at this giant number of bicycles parked in Amsterdam! Next, we traveled to Brussels in Belgium, which is a truly beautiful city. We spent a few hours sightseeing and eating delicious Belgian waffles that my mom bought us. Standing in front of the European Parliament building in Brussels. Inside the amazing Brussels Central Station. A classic, delicious Belgian waffle we had in the city center. After sightseeing, my mom drove me to a local climbing gym for a training session. I thought that climbing on foreign walls, where the route setting style is different than in Poland, would be great for developing my climbing skills. I wasn't disappointed at all – the gym was awesome! Later, we traveled to Normandy in France. We visited some very important historical places, like the American Cemetery, Omaha Beach, and the D-Day Museum. The American Cemetery in Normandy. It was a very quiet and serious place. A beautiful sunset at Omaha Beach. From Normandy, we went south to Paris. We did a lot of sightseeing there: we went to Trocadéro, saw the Eiffel Tower, the Sacré-Cœur basilica, and the famous Louvre museum. Me with the amazing Eiffel Tower glowing in the background at night. After exploring Paris, we went to Fontainebleau. It is a forest near the city and probably the most famous bouldering area in the world. I tried to send the famous dynamic boulder called "Rainbow Rocket". Unfortunately, I didn't manage to do it this time. But honestly, I am glad because now I have a great reason to go back! After all the amazing experiences on this trip, I definitely want to return there someday.

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: Surviving Exams and Finding My Place
posts.excerpt: After writing my 8th-grade exams, I was absolutely sure I had no chance of getting into Reytan. It is one of the top 20 high schools in Warsaw, and I thought I had completely ruined my chances because I started studying at the very last minute.
posts.body: After writing my 8th-grade exams, I was absolutely sure I had no chance of getting into Reytan. It is one of the top 20 high schools in Warsaw, and I thought I had completely ruined my chances because I started studying at the very last minute. This is me in my suit, looking very stressed just before my very first exam (Polish). But to my surprise, things didn't go badly at all! I got 82% in Polish, 93% in Math, and a perfect 100% in English. Thanks to these scores, I got into my first-choice school with points to spare. I remember how incredibly happy and relieved I was that I didn't fail and got exactly where I wanted. At the beginning of the school year, I was super excited. However, after a few weeks of studying, I noticed how incredibly smart everyone in my class was. For the first few months, I honestly felt like I didn't deserve to be there. I thought I just wasn't good enough. Now, my mindset has changed. I realized that I'm not dumber than my classmates – my main problem is just my own laziness! I am trying to work on it to catch up with the other students, although it's not always easy. But at least I no longer feel like I don't belong in this school. I think my biggest issue is that sometimes I don't believe in myself enough, and because of that, I want to give up instead of fighting for my goals. I am definitely working on changing that!

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: The Great Bouldering League: My Road to the Semi-Finals
posts.excerpt: WLB stands for Wielka Liga Boulderowa (The Great Bouldering League). It is a series of amateur bouldering competitions organized at various climbing gyms all over Poland. To advance to the semi-finals, you had to be in the top 50 climbers in your category (for me, it was the Men's category).
posts.body: WLB stands for Wielka Liga Boulderowa (The Great Bouldering League). It is a series of amateur bouldering competitions organized at various climbing gyms all over Poland. To advance to the semi-finals, you had to be in the top 50 climbers in your category (for me, it was the Men's category). The rules are simple but demanding: there are 6 rounds in total, and each round has 35 boulders. They go from number 1 (the easiest) up to number 35 (extremely hard). However, only your results from your 3 best rounds count for the final ranking. Because of that, I decided to save my energy and only compete in exactly three rounds. If I did well, it would be enough. And my strategy worked! My friends and me fooling around at the Groto gym in Poznań during the first round of WLB. My first round was at the Groto climbing gym in Poznań. Out of 35 boulders, I got 31 tops and 3 zones. I was quite proud of this result! Focusing hard on a boulder at the Crux gym in Warsaw. The second round took place at Crux in Warsaw, where I managed to get 32 tops. My third and final qualification round was at Volt, also in Warsaw. I did even better there – I scored 33 tops and 1 zone! These scores easily put me in the top 50 men and gave me a ticket to the semi-finals. Me climbing during the WLB round at the Volt gym. The semi-final took place just a few days ago, on Saturday, February 28th, back at the Volt gym. It was a completely different game. There were only 25 boulders, but they were much, much harder. I fought hard and managed to get 20 tops and 2 zones. This placed me in 17th position. Only the top 6 climbers advanced to the finals, so my WLB journey ended there. Honestly, it was a great experience. I know what I need to improve, and in the next WLB edition (2026/27), I will do better. Who knows, maybe I will even make it to the finals and compete against the older guys!

posts.author: Michał Rukszan
posts.title: Hitting the Wall: National Team Qualifiers
posts.excerpt: On February 15th, I took part in the most important competition of my career so far: the qualifiers for the Polish National Bouldering Team.
posts.body:
On February 15th, I took part in the most important competition of my career so far: the qualifiers for the Polish National Bouldering Team. I had prepared for this event not only mentally, but I had also been training extremely hard for the last few years. I honestly believed that this time I would do much better than in previous national-level events. Unfortunately, I was deeply disappointed.

The boulders were unbelievably difficult. The main reason was that all competitors climbed the exact same routes. This meant I wasn't just competing against guys in my age group, but also against seniors, who are on a completely different level than younger climbers. Despite knowing this, I started the competition with high hopes, thinking everything would be fine. However, I quickly realized I just wasn't ready yet. Failing to climb the first few boulders really messed with my head. It got to my psyche so much that I actually started asking myself, "What am I even doing here?"

In the end, it was a brutal reality check. I managed to get only 1 zone out of 6 boulders. I finished in second-to-last place out of all the competitors in the men's category. It was a very painful experience, but it definitely showed me how much work I still have ahead of me if I want to compete with the best.
